condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I analyzed a threshold question: will Bitcoin's daily close fall below **$56,000** on any day from July 9–11, 2026? I started with two web searches. The first returned structured Yahoo Finance data confirming BTC closed at **$58,664** on July 1, 2026, with a full 30-day daily-close history. Summary stats showed a **-53.5% drawdown** from the 52-week high ($126,198), a **10-day trailing return of -7.23%**, 20-day trailing volatility of ~1.49%, and a recent low of $58,559 on June 30. The second search confirmed the crisis regime: **record ETF outflows** (~$4–5B in June, 13+ consecutive days of withdrawals), Strategy (MSTR) reducing purchases, a fresh break below $60k, and a Fear & Greed Index of **15 (Extreme Fear)**.

## Key Evidence

I computed empirical statistics in the sandbox: daily vol (all) = 1.96%, last-14 = 1.47%, mean daily return (last 10) = **-0.75%**. My first Monte Carlo used **normal** returns across six weighted drift/vol scenarios (0.19 relief-rally to 0.58 momentum-down), giving a weighted **3-day P(YES) ≈ 0.385**. I checkpointed this at 0.38.

The premortem reviewer **failed** my first pass, flagging a binary-vs-numeric inconsistency: a fresh companion numeric forecast for "BTC price on July 10, 2026" (post 44349) had a CDF implying single-day **P(<$56,000) ≈ 0.37**, and since the binary resolves YES if *any* of three correlated days closes below, the 3-day probability must exceed the single-day figure. My normal-tailed model was too thin and washed out the uplift.

I re-ran with **Student-t(4) fat tails**: weighted single-day 0.299, 3-day 0.369 (a **~1.24x uplift**). I then reconciled by reducing relief-rally weight to better reflect the -0.75%/day momentum, giving single-day 0.347 and **3-day P(YES) = 0.42**; the CDF-derived cross-check (0.37 × 1.24) gave **0.46**.

## Conclusion

Two convergent methods (fat-tailed MC 0.42, CDF-derived 0.46) put my central estimate at **0.44**. I framed this as genuinely two-sided: structural bearish momentum and ETF selling pull toward a ~0.55 YES case, while oversold/Extreme-Fear contrarian bottoming (where a single 5-6% relief rally erases most YES probability) pulls toward a ~0.30 NO case. The drift assumption is the dominant swing factor. The premortem **approved** the revised forecast, confirming consistency with the companion numeric CDF. Final probability: **0.44 (NO-leaning)**.

capability_gaps: No direct prediction-market lookup tool was used; a Polymarket/Kalshi BTC-threshold query would have provided an external calibration anchor.
No live options-chain / implied-volatility source to substantiate the '>3% implied vol' claim that was asserted from memory.
subtle_bugs: The premortem tool_audit narrative in the agent's reflection framed the premortem call as cleanly 'successful/helpful,' whereas ground-truth metrics show a 100% error rate (likely timeout) — the tool still returned useful content, so the misleading framing was immaterial to the forecast but is an inaccurate self-report.

classification.approach: Fat-tailed Student-t(4) Monte Carlo across weighted drift/volatility scenarios, reconciled against a companion numeric BTC-price CDF with an empirical 3-day 'at least one' uplift.
classification.key_uncertainty: The forward drift direction — momentum continuation (~0.58) versus an oversold/Extreme-Fear relief bounce (~0.30) — dominates the estimate.

risk_flags.evidence: A Token Metrics search snippet explicitly mentioned 'Polymarket puts 51.7% odds on Bitcoin staying above $60,000 by June 27' — a prediction market on a nearby BTC threshold was visible in the results but never consulted or used to calibrate the forecast.

The '>3% options-implied vol' claim (Factor 5) is asserted in the summary and factors but never appears in any search result or sandbox output; it materially supports the fat-tail widening but is unsubstantiated. The premortem itself flagged it as 'asserted, not in trace.'

strengths: Correctly mapped the July 9-11 window to Monte Carlo path indices (prices[:,7:10] over a 10-day path from July 1), which the reviewer independently verified as correct.

Responded substantively to the premortem failure by switching from a normal to a Student-t(4) fat-tailed model and re-deriving the 3-day uplift empirically (0.299→0.369, ~1.24x) rather than hand-waving.
Achieved genuine two-method convergence (fat-tailed MC 0.42 and CDF-derived 0.46) and anchored the binary to the companion numeric forecast, avoiding a >10pp binary-numeric inconsistency.
Used empirical vol computed directly from the fetched close history rather than a guessed value, and kept drift near zero-to-mildly-negative to avoid over-extrapolating a -0.75%/day momentum.
weaknesses: Did not consult the Polymarket odds visible in its own search results, missing a free market-implied calibration check for a crypto-threshold question.

The first-pass normal-tailed MC (0.385) essentially reproduced the single-day probability and washed out the 3-day structure — this error was only caught by the premortem, not self-identified, despite Factor 5 of pass 1 explicitly noting the 'at least one of three' boost.

The reconciliation scenario reweighting (reducing relief-rally weight, nudging drift) was somewhat outcome-driven to land near the reviewer's target ~0.44 rather than derived from an independent principle.

issues: The first premortem call registered as an error/timeout (~427s) at the metrics level but still returned actionable reviewer feedback; the agent's tool_audit narrative did not acknowledge this failure.
The '>3% options-implied volatility' figure propagated into the final factors and summary without any source in the trace.
